We then push the lung posteriorly to do the anterior dissection of the superior pulmonary vein.

The peribronchial lymph nodes are dissected up towards the specimen for oncologic reasons, and this also aids in clearing of the bronchus for the eventual stapler.

Typically start with a fissure dissection and begin anteriorly on the oblique or major fissure.

In contrast, the anterior aspect of the oblique fissure dividing the middle lobe from the lower lobe can be a very good plane to start the initial identification of the pulmonary artery.

We do not dive into the fissure, but rather readily convert our approach to a fissureless anterior to posterior approach much like a VATS lobectomy.

This is one of the most difficult lobectomies to perform because of the variability of the segmental PA branches and the limited visibility of a short, broad-based anterior segmental artery as it passes beneath the left upper lobe pulmonary vein.
As with most robotic resections, we start with division of the inferior pulmonary ligament and removal of level IX lymph nodes.
The lung is then retracted anteriorly and the level 7 and 10L lymph node dissection is performed.
While the lung is retracted anteriorly, we do do some dissection of the pulmonary artery and the posterior fissure to open up this space.
The posterior dissection along the PA is critical and we stress that this is a fundamental principle of robotic lobectomy that the posterior dissection should be performed early and completely
to allow easy passage of the stapler from the front later during the vascular and fissure divisions.

and then this is divided using a stapler so as to minimize air leaks.

Once the pulmonary artery branches are isolated, we subsequently divide them with the use of a stapler from an anterior port.

For the superior dissection of the anterior and apical pulmonary artery branches, we switch to a 30 degree scope for a better top-down view.
The anterior branch of the pulmonary artery is then dissected out and subsequently divided.
The left upper lobe is then lifted up superiorly to expose the left upper lobe bronchus for division from a posterior stapling port.

Care is taken to not injure the recurrent laryngeal nerve or the phrenic nerve, which are in close proximity, and this is facilitated particularly with the robotic floor platform with the use of bipolar instrumentation that limits thermal spread.
We will now review left lower lobectomy.
In contrast to left upper lobectomy, left lower lobectomy is typically easier to perform because of more consistent vasculature and a smaller number of vessels that need ligation.
The exposure to the pulmonary artery vessels are also more straightforward with a left lower lobectomy.
We again start by dissection of the inferior pulmonary ligament and removal of level 9 and any level 8 lymph nodes encountered.
The inferior pulmonary ligament and mediastinal pleura are carried up superiorly until the inferior pulmonary vein is encountered and dissected out.
We then push the lung anteriorly to do the posterior dissection of the inferior pulmonary vein as well as the posterior mediastinal pleura for level 7 lymph node dissection.

As with left upper lobectomy, we do do superior posterior hilar dissection above the left pulmonary artery and remove level 10 lymph nodes in this area that assist with the eventual division of the posterior fissure later.
We then go to the fissure and begin our pulmonary artery dissection.
For the pulmonary artery dissection, we dissect right on the PA and raise a hood of parenchyma that subsequently will get stapled.
A prior posterior dissection above the pulmonary artery provides a nice landing zone for the stapler when dividing the posterior fissure.
Typically, several staple fires are required to complete the posterior fissure.
With the left lower lobe retracted inferiorly, we remove a level 11 lymph node that exposes the basilar PA branch more clearly.

If one cannot find the PA in the fissure, the lung can be retracted posteriorly and an anterior hilar dissection will reliably find the same 11L node, which is always there and a landmark for the PA and the underlying secondary carina.
In this patient, we were able to take both the basilar PA trunk and the superior segmental pulmonary artery together.
Sometimes these two branches do have to be taken separately.
We then retracted the lower lobe superiorly and isolated and divided the left lower lobe pulmonary vein, which had already been dissected.
We then lift the left lower lobe and divide the left lower lobe bronchus.
We do typically do a brief test inflation to make sure that the left upper lobe bronchiolumen is not obstructed.
The specimen is subsequently removed.
